audit_status.review_note: Reviewed as Job's opening lament: curse of birth-day, night of conception, Leviathan imagery, death/rest language, and anguished why-questions. Poetic lines are compact but verse-complete.

verses.restored: After this, Job opened his mouth and cursed his day.
Job answered and said:
Let the day perish on which I was born, and the night that said, A male has been conceived.
Let that day be darkness. Let the Divine above not seek it, and let no light shine upon it.
Let darkness and deep shadow reclaim it. Let a cloud dwell upon it. Let the blackness of day terrify it.
That night, let thick darkness take it. Let it not rejoice among the days of the year; let it not come into the number of months.
Look, let that night be barren; let no joyful cry enter it.
Let those who curse the day curse it, those ready to rouse Leviathan.
Let the stars of its dawn be dark. Let it wait for light and have none, and let it not see the eyelids of morning.
Because it did not shut the doors of my mother’s womb, nor hide trouble from my eyes.
Why did I not die from the womb, come out from the belly and expire?
Why did knees receive me, and why breasts, that I should nurse?
For now I would have lain down and been quiet; I would have slept; then I would be at rest,
with kings and counselors of the earth, who built ruins for themselves,
or with princes who had gold, who filled their houses with silver.
Or why was I not like a hidden miscarriage, like infants who never saw light?
There the wicked cease from agitation, and there the weary of strength rest.
Captives are at ease together; they do not hear the voice of the taskmaster.
Small and great are there, and the servant is free from his master.
Why does he give light to the troubled, and life to the bitter of soul,
who wait for death, but it is not there, and dig for it more than hidden treasures,
who rejoice greatly and are glad when they find the grave?
Why give light to a man whose way is hidden, whom the Divine has hedged in?
For my sighing comes before my bread, and my groanings pour out like waters.
For the dread I dreaded has come upon me, and what I feared has arrived at me.
I was not at ease, I was not quiet, I did not rest, and turmoil came.
